title: interface.v1 — a protocol contract a swappable implementation satisfies
description: One interface declaration: the stable callable boundary that lets the engine keep a lightweight built-in default while a heavier capability can slot in behind the same boundary, without the caller knowing which it got. A declaration names its operations (each with the shape of its input and output) and names its own default/fallback implementation, so degradability is stated at the contract rather than scattered into callers. This governs the slug-named files under .engine/interfaces/. An interface governs a PROTOCOL (a callable boundary); it is distinct from a schema (which governs a data payload's shape) and from the module-system wiring seam (which edits shared state). Implementations are discovered by presence and resolve single-active — exactly one answers; with none present the named fallback answers; more than one non-default present is a coherence finding. This schema fixes the declaration's shape; the per-operation input/output shapes are authored inline in each declaration.

schema_version.description: The interface-declaration format's version stamp, so a future interface.v2 migration can recognise and transform an old declaration. The only valid value is 1.

id.description: The interface's stable name — a lowercase kebab-case slug matching the declaration's filename stem (e.g. 'knowledge-retrieval', 'search'). It is the identity callers and implementations bind against; in the knowledge graph it appears as 'interface:<id>'.

title.description: A short human-readable label for the capability this interface provides, in plain language (e.g. 'Knowledge graph retrieval').

description.description: What the capability is and what a caller can ask of it, in plain language — not the slug, not implementation detail. This is what an operator or a cold-booting session reads to understand the seam.

status.description: Where this interface is in its lifecycle (the 'artifact' lifecycle). 'active' is in force; 'deprecated' is on its way out but still answered; 'retired' is no longer offered.

operations.description: The operation set every conforming implementation provides — the fallback floor and any richer swap-in alike. Each operation names itself, says what it does, and carries the JSON Schema of its input and of its output (the per-operation shapes, authored inline here). At least one operation is required; operation names must be unique within the declaration.

fallback.description: The default/fallback implementation this contract names, so the engine always has a working answer even with no module installed. For the v1 MCP-backed interfaces this is the engine-prefixed MCP server that ships as the conforming foundation floor; a richer module overrides it by presence at the same handle. The named fallback being present is what the conformance audit verifies; a protocol-only interface whose fallback ships with a later module names it here and is an expected pending state until that module lands.

kind.description: How the fallback is reached. For v1 the interfaces are MCP-backed: 'mcp' means the handle is an engine-prefixed MCP server name.

handle.description: The engine-namespaced handle of the fallback implementation. For kind 'mcp' this is the engine-prefixed MCP server name (e.g. 'engine-knowledge-graph') that a richer implementation overrides by presence at the same name.

operation.description: One operation in the contract: its name, a plain-language description, and the JSON Schema of its input and output. The input/output schemas are themselves JSON Schemas (objects), validated structurally by this grammar; their internal correctness is a conformance/test concern, not enforced here.

name.description: The operation's name — a lowercase kebab-case identifier unique within the interface (e.g. 'get-entity', 'find', 'neighbors', 'relate').

description.description: What the operation does and what it returns, in plain language.

input_schema.description: The JSON Schema of the operation's input (an object schema). Holds arbitrary JSON Schema keywords; not constrained further here.

output_schema.description: The JSON Schema of the operation's output (an object schema). Holds arbitrary JSON Schema keywords; not constrained further here.
